column6: Please note that the Neuter Gender and Related Definintion have been omitted, although Clearly Printed on the Page! Therefore, please ADD the Following: "(-ṣṭham) the bow of Karṇa & Kāma."

column7: Typo – Please note that the previous correction has added this Neuter  Gender Definition to the Previous Headword, so as to Match the Printed Page. Therefore, this listing may well be DELETED, as the Neuter Form now correctly is placed under the Primary Masculine listing. It appears that the standard method for AP is NOT to list the Masculine and Neuter Gender as TWO Separate Headwords. However, if you would like to proceed in this manner, please let me know so that I may bring other such cases to your attention, one way or the other.

Please note that this is an Indisputable Error by MW. Andhrabharati has confirmed that MW Dictionary is simply a copied translation of Dr. Böhtlingk's own German dictionary. Therefore, we should consult Dr. Böhtlingk's own listing of this very Headword; and here is what we find (with Google Translation): "pañcāsubandhura Adj. in whom the five life breaths form the chariot straps (?) Bhāg. P. 4,29,18. [ID=62203]." LB The Verse Citation Given from Bhāgavatam is also Indisputable. In fact this is a very well known allegory. The Five Horses are symbolic of the Five Senses; the Body is likened to a Chariot; and Here we come to this Grave Error of MW. The proper word is "pañcāsubandhura" as cited by Dr. Böhtlingk. Given the Indisputable Context of this usage, the Five Life Airs are likened to the Five Ropes connected between the Chariot (as body) and the Five Horses (as the senses). Therefore the following Correction/Revision MUST be made to MW Dictionary Online at Cologne as follows: LB "pañcāsu-vandhura mfn. whose carriage-seats (?) are the 5 vital airs, BhP." LB MUST BE CORRECTED TO READ LB "pañcāsu-bandhura mfn. whose ropes are the 5 vital airs, BhP." LB (Of course the word 'rope' could be 'cord' or whatever you prefer, but this correction must be made – there is no other option).
